player-addnotification-5.summary: Pushes a notification onto a specific player's notification stack.
notes: Use this when gameplay code needs to surface a specific event to one player without opening a full popup.
Pass -1 for x and y when the notification is not tied to a world plot.
Choose the notification type carefully because the game uses it to decide icon behavior and click handling.

example.summary: Notify the active player about something that happened at their capital.
example.code:
local iPlayer = Game.GetActivePlayer()
local pPlayer = Players[iPlayer]
local pCapital = pPlayer:GetCapitalCity()
if pCapital then
	pPlayer:AddNotification(
		NotificationTypes.NOTIFICATION_GENERIC,
		"A scripted effect resolved in your capital.",
		"Scripted Event",
		pCapital:GetX(),
		pCapital:GetY()
	)
end

gotchas: Some notification types expect extra payload data and will feel broken if iData and iDataExtra are always left at defaults.
Notification text should already be localized before it reaches this method.

seeAlso.note: Common way to resolve the local player before sending a notification.

player-cantrain-42.summary: Checks whether a player is currently allowed to train a unit type under the present game state.
notes: Use this before exposing custom production shortcuts or auto-queue logic that assumes the player can legally build a unit.
This is a player-wide legality check, so it is a good fit for civ-wide rules, policy locks, and resource gate logic.
If you need city-specific availability checks, pair it with city-side production logic instead of relying on this call alone.

example.summary: Guard a UI action before trying to queue a unit for the active player.
example.code:
local iPlayer = Game.GetActivePlayer()
local pPlayer = Players[iPlayer]
local iUnit = GameInfoTypes.UNIT_ARCHER
if pPlayer and pPlayer:CanTrain(iUnit, false, true, false, false) then
	print("Active player can currently train an Archer")
end

gotchas: The boolean flags materially change the answer, so do not cargo-cult a single flag set into every caller.
A true result here does not mean a specific city can produce the unit this turn; city context may still block it.

relatedSchemaNotes.note: This hook keys directly off unit rows, so trait overrides and production rules usually start here.
Check class-level replacement relationships when a civ swaps one unit row for another.

seeAlso.note: Event-side veto hook for adding extra train rules.
Inspect the unit rows and prereqs behind the queried type.

city-getnumbuilding-178.summary: Returns the current count of a specific building type in a city.
notes: Use this to test whether a city already has a building before granting yields, promotions, or dummy-building side effects.
For most standard buildings the answer is 0 or 1, but dummy-building patterns can intentionally stack counts above one.
This is usually the cleanest city-level read when you need an actual building row instead of a building class abstraction.

example.summary: Check whether the selected city already has a Monument row applied.
example.code:
local iBuilding = GameInfoTypes.BUILDING_MONUMENT
local pCity = UI.GetHeadSelectedCity()
if pCity and pCity:GetNumBuilding(iBuilding) > 0 then
	print("The selected city already has a Monument")
end

gotchas: The parameter is a BuildingType row ID, not a BuildingClass.
Dummy-building systems may legitimately return values greater than one.

relatedSchemaNotes.note: Look here first for per-building yield, specialist, and prerequisite data.
Class rows matter when unique buildings replace a shared class.

seeAlso.note: Use the construct hook when you need to block or allow a building before it enters production.

unit-canairliftat-12.summary: Tests whether a unit can airlift to the target location right now.
notes: Use this as a legality check before issuing scripted relocation behavior that depends on airport-style movement.
The plot object and x or y coordinates together give you both the destination context and the map position the game will validate.
This is best used as a yes or no gate before you attempt any UI or mission-side follow-up.

example.summary: Check whether the selected unit can airlift to its current plot coordinates.
example.code:
local pUnit = UI.GetHeadSelectedUnit()
if pUnit then
	local pPlot = pUnit:GetPlot()
	if pPlot and pUnit:CanAirliftAt(pPlot, pPlot:GetX(), pPlot:GetY()) then
		print("Selected unit has a valid airlift target check")
	end
end

gotchas: Airlift legality depends on both source and destination state, not just whether the coordinates are reachable on the map.
A false result does not explain which airport or unit-state rule blocked the move.

seeAlso.note: Useful when the airlift check is being driven from active-player UI code.

game-getactiveplayer-44.summary: Returns the player ID currently treated as active by the running game or UI context.
notes: In UI code this is usually the local human player, and the usual next step is Players[Game.GetActivePlayer()].
This is a stable helper for front-end and in-game UI code that needs a player handle without already carrying one around.
It is also a common bridge into player-scoped methods like notifications, production lookups, and diplomacy UI.

example.summary: Resolve the active player object before calling other player-scoped helpers.
example.code:
local iPlayer = Game.GetActivePlayer()
local pPlayer = Players[iPlayer]
if pPlayer then
	print("Active player ID:", iPlayer)
end

gotchas: Do not assume the active player is the same thing as the turn owner in every UI flow or multiplayer situation.
This returns an ID, not a Player object, so callers still need to index into Players.

seeAlso.note: Common follow-up once you have the active player object.
Do not confuse active-player UI context with the per-player turn hook.

player-getcapitalcity-141.summary: Returns the player's current capital city object, or nil if the player no longer controls one.
notes: Use this when a script needs a stable anchor city for notifications, wonder side effects, or empire-wide calculations that should start at the capital.
This returns a City object directly, so it is often a cleaner first hop than resolving city IDs and then walking the player city list.
Pair it with nil checks because capitals can be lost, razed, or not yet founded in some scenario states.

example.summary: Resolve the active player's capital before applying a capital-scoped effect.
example.code:
local iPlayer = Game.GetActivePlayer()
local pPlayer = Players[iPlayer]
local pCapital = pPlayer and pPlayer:GetCapitalCity()
if pCapital then
	print("Capital city:", pCapital:GetName())
	print("Capital coordinates:", pCapital:GetX(), pCapital:GetY())
end

gotchas: This returns a City object, not a city ID.
The result can be nil even for a living player if the capital has been lost or never founded.

seeAlso.note: Common first step before resolving the current player's capital.
Often paired with capital coordinates when sending map-tied notifications.

game-getgameturn-80.summary: Returns the current game turn index used by the running match.
notes: Use this for lightweight timing checks, cooldown comparisons, and persistence keys that need a stable turn marker.
This is a better fit than calendar strings when logic only cares about sequence and not display year formatting.
It is especially useful in recurring hooks like PlayerDoTurn when you want to avoid running the same effect twice in one turn.

example.summary: Compare the current turn to a saved marker before running an effect again.
example.code:
local iTurn = Game.GetGameTurn()
print("Current turn:", iTurn)

local lastProcessedTurn = Modding.OpenSaveData().GetValue("CMC_LastProcessedTurn")
if lastProcessedTurn ~= iTurn then
	print("This turn has not been processed yet")
end

gotchas: The turn number is not the same thing as the displayed world year.
Do not confuse a turn counter with active-player UI context; many players can still be processed during the same turn.

seeAlso.note: Natural recurring hook for turn-indexed gameplay effects.

unit-pushmission-336.summary: Pushes a mission command onto a unit's mission queue.
notes: Use this when a script should issue a real unit mission instead of directly mutating state and hoping the UI catches up later.
The mission type controls how iData1 and iData2 are interpreted, so callers should keep mission-specific argument construction explicit.
This method is a strong fit for generated Lua helpers that need to append or replace standard unit orders.

example.summary: Send the selected unit a simple skip mission.
example.code:
local pUnit = UI.GetHeadSelectedUnit()
if pUnit then
	pUnit:PushMission(MissionTypes.MISSION_SKIP)
end

gotchas: Mission argument meanings change by mission type, so one generic argument pattern will not work for every mission.
Pushing a mission does not guarantee the unit can legally perform it in the current state.

relatedSchemaNotes.note: Mission rows are the closest schema touchpoint for the mission type passed into the call.

seeAlso.note: Useful when mission logic depends on who owns the target plot.

plot-getowner-60.summary: Returns the owning player ID for a plot, or -1 when the plot is unowned.
notes: Use this when territory ownership matters more than plot visibility or city assignment.
This is a useful bridge from map coordinates back into player-scoped logic after resolving a plot from an event hook.
The return value is lightweight and easy to combine with Players[...] lookups or territory comparison checks.

example.summary: Resolve a plot owner after converting map coordinates into a plot object.
example.code:
local pPlot = Map.GetPlot(iPlotX, iPlotY)
if pPlot then
	local iOwner = pPlot:GetOwner()
	if iOwner ~= -1 then
		print("Plot owner:", Players[iOwner]:GetName())
	end
end

gotchas: The return value is a player ID, not a Player object.
Unowned plots return -1, so always guard before indexing into Players.

seeAlso.note: Natural event hook for reacting after plot ownership changes.

team-ishastech-110.summary: Checks whether a team currently owns a specific technology.
notes: Use this for empire-wide tech gates where team ownership matters more than one specific player handle.
This is the right layer for rules that should apply to every member of a team once the tech is unlocked.
It pairs naturally with tech-triggered event hooks and schema lookups into the Technologies table.

example.summary: Check whether the active player's team has Writing before allowing a tech-gated effect.
example.code:
local iPlayer = Game.GetActivePlayer()
local pPlayer = Players[iPlayer]
local pTeam = pPlayer and Teams[pPlayer:GetTeam()]
local iTech = GameInfoTypes.TECH_WRITING
if pTeam and pTeam:IsHasTech(iTech) then
	print("The active player's team already has Writing")
end

gotchas: This is a team-level check, not a player-level one.
The parameter is a technology enum row ID, so missing custom tech rows should be validated before the call.

relatedSchemaNotes.note: Use the technology row for prereqs, era placement, and display text tied to the gate.

seeAlso.note: Hook that fires when tech ownership changes and can trigger follow-up logic.

game-event-buildfinished-1.summary: Hook fired when a build action finishes on a plot and resolves into an improvement result.
notes: Use this after an improvement actually lands on the map, not while a worker is still deciding whether the action is legal.
The hook gives you player and plot coordinates, which is enough to resolve the plot and inspect terrain, owner, and follow-up effects.
This is a good place for post-build rewards, terrain cleanup, or progress systems that only care about finished work.

example.summary: Grant a simple log message when a worker build finishes.
example.code:
GameEvents.BuildFinished.Add(function(iPlayer, iPlotX, iPlotY, eImprovement)
	local pPlot = Map.GetPlot(iPlotX, iPlotY)
	if pPlot then
		print("Build finished for player", iPlayer, "at", iPlotX, iPlotY, eImprovement)
	end
end)

gotchas: The improvement parameter reflects the completed improvement row, not necessarily the original build action enum chosen by the unit.
If you need to reason about the worker action itself, you will often need to cross-check Builds as well as Improvements.

relatedSchemaNotes.note: Use the completed improvement row for yield changes, pillage rules, and upgrade behavior.
The worker action that produced the result is documented separately from the finished improvement row.

seeAlso.note: Cross-check build actions when the finished improvement alone is not enough context.

game-event-playercantrain-70.summary: Player-level hook for vetoing or permitting access to a unit type before production proceeds.
notes: Use this to inject extra civ-wide train rules without rewriting every city production surface yourself.
Keep handlers narrow and return quickly for players you do not intend to modify.
This hook is best for broad player state checks such as traits, eras, game options, or scenario rules.

example.summary: Block a specific unit type for minor civs.
example.code:
GameEvents.PlayerCanTrain.Add(function(iPlayer, eUnit)
	local pPlayer = Players[iPlayer]
	if pPlayer and pPlayer:IsMinorCiv() and eUnit == GameInfoTypes.UNIT_ARCHER then
		return false
	end
	return true
end)

gotchas: Treat false as a veto and avoid broad handlers that accidentally suppress unrelated unit types.
This hook does not tell you which city is asking, so it is the wrong place for local building or terrain requirements.

relatedSchemaNotes.note: This is the main row set for unit costs, prereqs, combat classes, and upgrade chains.
Check class replacement behavior when civ uniques redirect one unit row into another.

seeAlso.note: Method-side legality check for the same production question.

game-event-playercanconstruct-62.summary: Player-level hook for permitting or vetoing access to a building type across the player's empire.
notes: Use this for civ-wide or empire-wide building rules that should apply before a specific city gets involved.
This is a good place for policy, ideology, religion, or scenario checks that do not depend on one city's local state.
Return quickly for players outside your mod's target rules to keep the production pipeline predictable.

example.summary: Deny a building globally until a prerequisite policy branch is finished.
example.code:
GameEvents.PlayerCanConstruct.Add(function(iPlayer, eBuilding)
	local pPlayer = Players[iPlayer]
	if pPlayer and eBuilding == GameInfoTypes.BUILDING_GARDEN then
		return pPlayer:IsPolicyBranchFinished(GameInfoTypes.POLICY_BRANCH_TRADITION)
	end
	return true
end)

gotchas: This hook does not tell you which city is evaluating the building, so city-local restrictions belong elsewhere.
The building parameter is a BuildingType row ID, not a BuildingClass.

relatedSchemaNotes.note: Start here for yields, prereqs, classes, specialists, and wonder flags.
Class rows matter when uniques replace a shared base building.

seeAlso.note: Use the city-level hook when the restriction depends on one city's state.
Useful follow-up when validating city state around existing building counts.

game-event-citycanconstruct-22.summary: City-level hook for permitting or vetoing whether one specific city can construct a building type.
notes: Use this when the rule depends on city-local state such as terrain, local dummy buildings, conquest status, or nearby resources.
The city parameter is a city ID, so callers usually resolve the city through the owning player's city collection before inspecting it.
This hook is more precise than the player-level construct hook when only some cities should be affected.

example.summary: Hide a building unless the target city already has a prerequisite dummy building.
example.code:
GameEvents.CityCanConstruct.Add(function(iPlayer, iCity, eBuilding)
	local pPlayer = Players[iPlayer]
	local pCity = pPlayer and pPlayer:GetCityByID(iCity)
	if pCity and eBuilding == GameInfoTypes.BUILDING_GARDEN then
		return pCity:GetNumBuilding(GameInfoTypes.BUILDING_MONUMENT) > 0
	end
	return true
end)

gotchas: Community notes suggest a false return removes the building from the production list rather than showing it disabled with an explanation.
The building parameter is a BuildingType ID, so building-class replacement logic still needs to be handled explicitly.

relatedSchemaNotes.note: Inspect the target row here when your rule depends on building flags or costs.
Use class rows to reason about unique-building substitutions.

seeAlso.note: Use the player-level hook for empire-wide construct rules.
Common helper when the construct rule depends on existing building counts.

game-event-playerdoturn-72.summary: Per-player turn hook that fires during the turn cycle rather than only for the active human UI context.
notes: This is a strong default hook for per-player upkeep, counters, deferred maintenance, and scenario bookkeeping.
Modder experimentation on the wiki reports that it fires at the start of turn rather than after player actions resolve.
Use the passed player ID directly instead of assuming the active player matches the turn currently being processed.

example.summary: Run a once-per-turn maintenance check for every alive player.
example.code:
GameEvents.PlayerDoTurn.Add(function(iPlayer)
	local pPlayer = Players[iPlayer]
	if pPlayer and pPlayer:IsAlive() then
		print("Processing scripted upkeep for player", iPlayer)
	end
end)

gotchas: The wiki notes that this does not appear to run on the first turn of the game, only for the human player on turn two, and for all players after that.
Because it runs for every player, expensive logic here can scale poorly in large games.

seeAlso.note: Useful contrast when you need local UI context instead of the current turn owner.

game-event-cityboughtplot-17.summary: Hook fired after a city acquires a plot through gold, faith, or culture purchase logic.
notes: Use this after plot ownership has actually changed and the new plot can be resolved from map coordinates.
This is a strong fit for border-growth side effects, terrain-triggered bonuses, or city acquisition tracking.
The booleans help distinguish purchase pathways without requiring the hook to infer intent from the city state afterward.

example.summary: Log a newly purchased plot and the purchase path that was used.
example.code:
GameEvents.CityBoughtPlot.Add(function(iPlayer, iCity, iPlotX, iPlotY, bGold, bFaithOrCulture)
	local pPlot = Map.GetPlot(iPlotX, iPlotY)
	if pPlot then
		print("City bought plot:", iPlayer, iCity, iPlotX, iPlotY, bGold, bFaithOrCulture)
	end
end)

gotchas: The city parameter is a city ID, not a City object.
bFaithOrCulture only tells you the purchase came through the non-gold path; additional mod rules may still be needed to distinguish the exact source.

seeAlso.note: Useful follow-up when reacting to the new owner of the purchased plot.

game-event-canhavepromotion-5.summary: Unit-level veto hook for deciding whether a specific unit can receive a promotion.
notes: Use this to enforce custom promotion rules that depend on scenario logic, civ design, or special unit states.
The hook runs before the promotion is accepted, so it is a clean place to reject invalid promotion paths.
Because it only receives a unit ID, promotion gating logic should resolve the unit object explicitly when deeper inspection is needed.

example.summary: Prevent one promotion from being granted through the normal path.
example.code:
GameEvents.CanHavePromotion.Add(function(iPlayer, iUnit, ePromotion)
	if ePromotion == GameInfoTypes.PROMOTION_MARCH then
		return false
	end
	return true
end)

gotchas: Returning false is a veto, so broad handlers can accidentally suppress large parts of the promotion tree.
The unit argument is a unit ID, not a Unit object.

relatedSchemaNotes.note: Promotion rows define the actual promotion being filtered by the hook.

seeAlso.note: Related unit gating hook for upgrade paths rather than promotion ownership.

game-event-candeclarewar-2.summary: Team-level veto hook for whether one team can declare war on another team.
notes: Use this when diplomacy restrictions should apply before war is declared, not after the fact.
Because the hook works on team IDs, it is suitable for broad scenario rules and alliance-based restrictions.
This is a clean place to stop early-game wars, permanent peace rules, or scripted diplomacy locks.

example.summary: Block declarations of war before a chosen turn threshold.
example.code:
GameEvents.CanDeclareWar.Add(function(eFromTeam, eToTeam)
	if Game.GetGameTurn() < 50 then
		return false
	end
	return true
end)

gotchas: The hook uses team IDs, not player IDs.
A blanket false return can freeze diplomacy across the entire match if the handler is not narrowly scoped.

seeAlso.note: Simple timing helper for scenario-style war locks.
Useful when war permissions depend on tech ownership at the team level.

game-event-teamsethastech-81.summary: Hook fired when a team gains or loses ownership of a technology.
notes: Use this for tech-triggered rewards, notifications, or cleanup that should happen the moment a team tech state changes.
The hook provides the new boolean state directly, so the same callback can handle both acquisition and removal flows.
This is a strong fit for mods that unlock systems or grants when specific technologies come online.

example.summary: Log the tech transition when a team changes ownership of a technology.

gotchas: The hook can represent both gaining and losing a tech, so bValue should always be checked explicitly.
The team parameter is a team ID, not a Team object.

relatedSchemaNotes.note: Use the tech row for display text, prereqs, and downstream unlock relationships.

seeAlso.note: Method-side check for the same tech state after the hook fires.

game-event-canhaveupgrade-6.summary: Unit-level veto hook for whether a specific unit may upgrade into a target unit type and class.
notes: Use this to enforce custom upgrade restrictions that depend on civ rules, scenarios, or temporary gameplay states.
The hook provides both the unit class and the target unit row, which makes it useful for unique-unit replacement logic.
This is one of the cleaner places to stop an upgrade path before it appears to the player as valid.

example.summary: Disallow a specific upgrade target through the event layer.
example.code:
GameEvents.CanHaveUpgrade.Add(function(iPlayer, iUnit, eUnitClass, eUnit)
	if eUnit == GameInfoTypes.UNIT_MACHINE_GUN then
		return false
	end
	return true
end)

gotchas: The unit parameter is a unit ID, not a Unit object.
Returning false blocks the upgrade path entirely, so checks should stay tightly targeted.

relatedSchemaNotes.note: Target unit rows define what the upgrade is trying to become.
The class parameter helps when civ uniques remap the same class to different units.

seeAlso.note: Sibling unit gating hook when the restriction is about promotions rather than upgrades.
Related legality check on the production side for unit access.
